briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon, everyone. I will now begin the climate briefing for July 21, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Global Spread and Severity of the Climate Crisis
sections.content: World Heritage sites are in peril due to climate change and conflict [1], emphasizing the need for international cooperation and solidarity [12]. In Europe, extreme heatwaves threaten Italy's 800-year-old Parmesan cheese tradition [45], and Australian ski resorts have broken July climate records with temperatures up to 12°C above average [71]. In the Western U.S., the likelihood of snow drought has quadrupled this year [48], and concerns are rising that increased extreme wildfires due to climate change could make smoky summer days a new norm [46, 53]. Deteriorating weather conditions due to the climate crisis are also reported in the San Francisco Bay Area and Buffalo regions [42, 51], while in Iraq, 134 wildlife species are endangered due to climate change and pollution [62]. These are just some examples of the severe impacts of climate change manifesting globally [50, 70, 73].

sections.title: ⚠️ Urgency and Challenges of Domestic Climate Change Response
sections.content: Domestically, the distribution industry faces an 'ultra-emergency' situation due to abnormal weather, experiencing difficulties in product procurement and event planning [10]. Unpredictable weather phenomena continue, with strong rains accompanied by gales, thunder, and lightning expected primarily in the central region, while heatwaves persist in the southern regions and Jeju [39]. As the carbon neutrality deadline approaches, the display industry is under urgent pressure, with Samsung Display urgently requesting joint responses with its partners [26, 31]. Despite the importance of expanding renewable energy, concerns have been raised that resolving issues of grid confusion and insufficient capacity in the power system should be prioritized [36, 37]. Amidst these challenges, support projects such as consulting on sesame and strawberry cultivation techniques are being promoted in the domestic agricultural sector to respond to climate change [5, 7, 8, 6, 9].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Efforts and Progress Towards Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Global mandatory carbon neutrality certification procedures have been simplified, lowering the threshold for domestic companies to achieve their goals [14, 17], and the Korea Environment Corporation is accelerating efforts to secure international greenhouse gas reduction achievements [13]. Various local governments and institutions nationwide are actively conducting education and campaigns for carbon neutrality practices. Notable examples include programs for Gapyeong elementary and middle school students [15, 23], Yeoncheon-gun's core generations [16], Osan City businesses [18], Jeju National University's professional track field trips [19], and the Nammok Elementary School Children's Startup Competition [25]. The Incheon Institute hosted a forum to promote citizen-led carbon neutrality practices [20, 24, 28], and Hwasun-gun is encouraging participation through a carbon neutrality practice point system and local currency payments [21, 29]. Furthermore, Lotte E&C is strengthening its ESG competitiveness by publishing a sustainability management report that reflects international disclosure standards [11], and BEXCO and Net Zero Walk have presented a citizen participation model for achieving carbon neutrality through 72.5 million steps [22]. In the renewable energy sector, power standards are changing [32], with developments such as next-generation data centers combining renewable energy and AI computing [33], the initiation of discussions on reforming grid codes to accommodate renewable energy [34], and floating offshore wind power gaining attention as a key clean energy source in Ulsan [38]. These positive advancements continue [75].

sections.title: 🌍 Climate Science Research and Multifaceted International Discussions
sections.content: At the forefront of climate change monitoring, the value of long-term meteorological observations is being emphasized [4], and experts continue to research methods for measuring climate change [47, 55]. A Swiss study has shown that climate change exacerbates ground-level ozone pollution [60], and an analysis suggests that cloudy skies have played a role in slowing global warming [58]. New perspectives on species survival are also being explored, such as research on Southern Right Whale feces revealing that these animals may be more resilient to climate change than previously thought [57]. In the international community, various discussions are underway, including calls for investigations into climate change-related lawsuits [44, 49], strategies to garner Republican support for climate action [43], controversies surrounding NATO's alleged disregard for climate change threats [65], and pension funds' efforts to address global warming scenarios [66]. Meanwhile, innovative adaptation examples are reported, such as rainwater harvesting in Mexico City to cultivate gardens and supply households [74], and proactive responses from civil society are notable, with residents in Louisiana, USA, collecting air samples in response to harmful air pollution [72].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing demonstrates that continuous efforts toward solutions are ongoing, even amidst a global crisis. This is a critical time when international solidarity and scientific advancements are even more imperative for addressing climate change. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.original_title: Slowdown in Arctic ice melt ends after largest winter decline on record
articles.description: Scientists recorded slower reduction over past 20 years, but study finds this was ‘temporary rather than sustained’A previously recorded slowdown in Arctic sea ice decline has ended, according to a new study, with the largest year-to-year wintertime reduction ever recorded.In a study published last summer, researchers found that melting sea ice in the Arctic had slowed down over the past 20 years, with no statistically significant decline in its extent recorded between 2005 and 2024. articles.original_title: Afghanistan floods kill at least 20 people, with more than 100 missing
articles.description: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in remote province of NuristanAt least 20 people have been killed and more than 80 injured in flooding that hit Afghanistan’s Nuristan province, the country’s disaster management agency said on Monday.In Parun, the provincial capital, a further 105 people including the mayor were missing, said Feraidon Samim, a spokesperson for the Nuristan governor. Continue reading...

articles.title: Australian ski resorts smash July climate records with temperatures 12C above average
articles.description: Resorts in New South Wales and Victoria face a warm and dry start to the season with only sporadic falls of natural snow in many alpine areasFollow our Australia news live blog for latest updatesGet our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcastAlpine ski resorts in New South Wales and Victoria saw climate records shattered at the weekend with maximum temperatures up to 12C above average.Ski areas of Mount Hotham and Falls Creek set July records for their hottest daytime and night-time

summary.phenomenon: In July 2023, ski resorts in New South Wales and Victoria, Australia, shattered climate records by experiencing maximum temperatures up to 12°C above average. Specifically, Mount Hotham and Falls Creek set new July records for their hottest daytime and nighttime temperatures, leading to a warm and dry start to the season with only sporadic natural snowfall in many alpine areas.
summary.outlook: While a warm and dry start to the ski season is anticipated, the article does not specifically mention long-term impacts or future outlooks.

articles.title: ‘A public health emergency waiting to occur’: how US industry groups spent years fighting who gets to measure toxic air
articles.description: Air polluters try to ensure toxic emissions happen far from monitors – but the people who live nearby still feel itKimbrelle Kyereh lives just a few hundred feet from the massive chemical storage tanks in St Rose, in south-eastern Louisiana. Oily fumes sometimes drift into her home, bringing headaches, nausea and insomnia. On one such night, she grabbed her son at 3am and fled to an all-night Ihop.In 2023, after years of coping with the noxious odors, Kyereh began collecting air samples in her y

summary.phenomenon: Residents in St. Rose, southeastern Louisiana, USA, living near massive chemical storage tanks, are experiencing headaches, nausea, and insomnia due to oily fumes drifting into their homes. In 2023, resident Kimbrelle Kyereh began collecting air samples in response to these noxious odors.
summary.cause: This phenomenon is attributed to 'toxic emissions' originating from 'air polluters' (industrial facilities) located near residential areas. The lack of adequate monitoring or regulation is also implicated, given that industries have debated hazardous air measurement methods for years.
summary.outlook: This situation is described as a 'public health emergency waiting to happen,' suggesting potential future health crises for residents. Furthermore, there is a possibility that industries will continue to influence air quality measurements.

articles.title: ‘My shirt was wringing’: National Express bus drivers speak out on 40C conditions
articles.description: Drivers in West Midlands say buses ‘unbearable’ during heatwaves, raising employee and passenger safety fearsBus drivers in the West Midlands have spoken out about the “horrendous” working conditions they have experienced during heatwaves, which led to 40C temperatures in their cabs.The UK was hit by high temperatures and dry weather in three successive heatwaves, with temperatures topping 37C last month when the country recorded its hottest ever June day. Last week, the Met Office warned climat

summary.phenomenon: Bus drivers in the West Midlands, UK, experienced 'horrendous' working conditions during heatwaves, with temperatures in their cabs reaching 40°C. This occurred during a period when the UK endured three successive heatwaves, recording its hottest ever June day with temperatures exceeding 37°C.
summary.cause: This phenomenon is due to 'hot, dry weather and three successive heatwaves' that impacted the UK. The UK Met Office's warning about climate change suggests that climate change is an underlying factor contributing to these extreme weather events.
summary.outlook: Extreme temperatures are raising 'concerns for employee and passenger safety.' The Met Office's climate warning implies the potential for similar or worsening situations to occur in the future.

articles.title: Prickly pears, avocados and lemons: how rainwater harvesting is feeding Mexico City’s gardens and homes
articles.description: Thousands of low-income households are benefiting from a simple system that captures and filters rainfall from the city’s roofsWalking up a steep hill to a small informal settlement of about 45 houses in Cristo Rey, above San Gregorio Atlapulco in Mexico City, Ana Aparicio Bahena arrives at her simple home. As she walks through her lush, verdant garden, she points out plants such as the prickly pear, cactus leaves with tiny, hair-like spines, and picks blackberries hanging from a bush, handing t

summary.phenomenon: Thousands of low-income households, such as those in Cristo Rey, Mexico City, are cultivating gardens with various plants like prickly pear, avocado, and lemon, using a simple system that collects and filters rainwater from their roofs.
summary.cause: This phenomenon has been made possible by a 'rainwater harvesting' system that collects and filters rainwater to supply water. The article does not specifically mention the scientific reasons or background for adopting this system.
summary.outlook: Thousands of low-income households are benefiting, which is expected to improve water access for gardening and potentially household use, contributing to food security and better living conditions.
